quotes.text: A firm can have value only if it ultimately delivers earnings.
Success in investing comes not from being right but from being wrong less often than everyone else.
Einstein was right about relativity, but even he would have had a difficult time applying relative valuation in today's stock markets.
Growth requires reinvestment.
The intrinsic value of an asset is determined by the cash flows you expect that asset to generate over its life and how uncertain you feel about these cash flows.
Do not be afraid to make mistakes.
Growth firms get more of their value from investments that they expect to make in the future and less from investments already made.
I am naturally drawn to numbers but one of the ironies of working with numbers is that the more I work with them, the more skeptical I become about purely number-driven arguments.
We relate to and remember stories better than we do numbers, but storytelling can lead us into fantasyland quickly.
Great growth companies can be bad investments at the wrong price.
Will you be wrong sometimes? Of course, but so will everyone else.
More value is destroyed around the world by companies not acting their age: young companies trying to act old and old companies trying to be young again.
A good valuation is a bridge between stories and numbers.
The right price: Great growth companies can be bad investments at the wrong price.
In investing, the right answer is less important than the right process.
The market is not always right, but it is always the market.
Value investing is not about buying stocks that are cheap relative to book value or earnings, but about buying stocks that are cheap relative to their intrinsic value.
The biggest risk in valuation is not being wrong, but being precisely wrong.
In the long term, earnings and cash flows matter more than stories.
Diversification is protection against ignorance. It makes little sense if you know what you are doing.

author: Aswath Damodaran
description: Quotes from the renowned NYU finance professor and valuation expert Aswath Damodaran, known as the 'Dean of Valuation'.
